question: How do you feel when you are given a blank canvas, clay, or a blank file with no instructions?

options.text: Excited—I love making something new with no rules.
I would look for ideas online first, then add my own touch.
I prefer having a basic template or guide to start.
Frustrated—I work best with clear rules and a defined goal.

question: How do you organize your workspace, files, and tasks when starting a new project?

options.text: I make organized folders, clear checklists, and a set timeline.
I set up a basic organization system and change it if needed.
I keep things in one place and organize them only when necessary.
I start working immediately without organizing first.

question: If you start a small weekend business with friends, what role do you take?

options.text: The Seller—talking to customers and making sales.
The Planner—organizing the tasks and telling others what to do.
The Worker—doing the main work behind the scenes.
The Helper—assisting quietly with whatever tasks I am given.

question: What do you do when you see an error message or a problem in a system?

options.text: I read guides and forums to find the exact cause of the problem.
I test different things one by one to find the issue.
I search online for a quick fix that worked for others.
I ask an expert or find a different way around the problem.

question: How do you prefer to handle a broken household appliance or device?

options.text: I take it apart and try to fix it myself with tools.
I look up a video or guide to see if I can fix it easily.
I prefer fixing software issues; I do not like physical repairs.
I call a professional or buy a new one immediately.

question: How would you like to spend a Saturday afternoon working on a personal project?

options.text: Teaching others, helping a friend, or working directly with a team.
Working in a shared room where I can talk with teammates.
Working on my own but talking with a small group sometimes.
Working completely alone in a quiet room with no interruptions.

question: What do you do when you see news about a breakthrough outside your field?

options.text: Spend time reading articles and searching for the research paper just because it is interesting.
Read a quick summary to understand the general idea.
Save it for later, though I might not actually read it.
Ignore it; I only focus on information that helps my daily work.

question: What do you do if you notice a slow part in your team's workflow that others have not mentioned?

options.text: Create a solution or write down a better process on my own and show it to the team.
Mention it at the next meeting and offer to help fix it.
Talk casually with a coworker about it to see what they think.
Wait for a manager to handle it; I prefer to focus only on my own tasks.

question: What do you do when a group project starts with no clear plan or leader?

options.text: I step up to lead, make a plan, and assign tasks to others.
I offer to lead if no one else wants to.
I let someone else lead while I focus on my own tasks.
I avoid leading; I work better when someone else is in charge.

question: What do you do when you have tried to fix a problem for days and kept failing?

options.text: I keep trying new ways, determined to solve it no matter how long it takes.
I take a quick break, then try again with a fresh approach.
I will try a bit longer, but I will change direction if I stay stuck.
I stop and ask for help immediately, or move to an easier task.

question: How do you review your work before turning it in?

options.text: I check it line-by-line multiple times to find any small mistakes.
I look over the main points and double-check the most important parts.
I do a quick scan to make sure it looks good and works.
I submit it as soon as the main work is done; small mistakes can be fixed later.

question: How do you react when project requirements change completely halfway through?

options.text: I change direction immediately and easily find a new way forward.
I feel frustrated at first, but then I update my plan.
I find it hard to change at first, but I eventually adapt.
I hate when things change; I prefer clear and stable guidelines.

question: How do you prefer to solve a standard problem?

options.text: I try to invent a brand new method that no one has tried before.
I look at existing solutions and try to make them better.
I use standard, proven methods that are known to work.
I follow the exact step-by-step instructions of a guide.

question: Why do you work hard and stay up late on a project?

options.text: To reach high goals and push my personal standards.
The satisfaction of doing a good job and getting recognized.
To complete my duties and make sure the project works well.
Just to meet the basic requirements and keep a good work-life balance.

question: How do you handle sharing tasks and decisions in a team?

options.text: I prioritize agreement, seek group consensus, and share the work equally.
I collaborate well, but I prefer clear boundaries for everyone's tasks.
I prefer to work on my section alone, checking in only when necessary.
I prefer complete control over my work; working with others slows me down.

question: How important is socializing and making friends with coworkers to you?

options.text: Very important—I work best when I chat and connect with others.
Important—I enjoy talking to others, but I focus mostly on the work.
Not very important—I prefer to keep conversations strictly professional.
I avoid social chit-chat; I just want to do my work and go home.

question: How do you react during a major crisis with tight deadlines and panic around you?

options.text: I remain completely calm and focused on finding a quick solution.
I feel the pressure, but I can manage my stress and keep working.
My stress spikes, and I need a few moments to calm down before I can think.
I easily get overwhelmed; I work much better when there is no intense pressure.

question: How do you react to harsh, blunt criticism about your work?

options.text: I listen calmly, control my emotions, and try to learn from the feedback.
I feel defensive at first, but I manage to stay calm and say thank you.
I find it hard to hide my frustration or disappointment.
I argue back immediately; I cannot stay quiet under criticism.

question: How confident do you feel when presenting your work to a group of experts?

options.text: Completely confident—I know my work is good and can defend my choices.
Mostly confident, though I still feel a bit nervous.
I need praise from others to feel good about my work.
Very anxious—I constantly doubt my own knowledge and choices.

question: How do you make an important decision that has potential risks?

options.text: I spend a lot of time gathering data and checking risks before deciding.
I do some basic planning, then proceed carefully.
I trust my instincts and make a quick choice to avoid wasting time.
I jump right in; I would rather deal with problems later than hesitate now.

question: How do you feel about open-ended tasks with vague instructions?

options.text: Excited—I love the freedom to decide my own path.
Comfortable, as long as I can check in sometimes to see if I am doing it right.
A bit anxious—I prefer having at least some examples or clear steps.
Stressed—I need exact, step-by-step instructions to do the work.

question: How easily can you apply general rules or policies to a specific situation?

options.text: Very easily—I can instantly see how the rules apply to a specific case.
With some focus, I can connect the rules to a specific case.
I sometimes struggle to apply abstract rules to real situations.
I find it confusing; I need someone to show me how the rule applies.

question: How quickly can you turn a spreadsheet or system layout into mathematical formulas?

options.text: Instantly—I naturally see the math and formulas in data systems.
Fairly well, though I have to write down the math to be sure.
Slowly—I can do basic math, but advanced modeling takes a lot of effort.
I struggle; I prefer simple descriptions over math formulas.

question: How quickly do you notice when a system or workflow has a small problem?

options.text: Immediately—I can easily spot small errors before they become major bugs.
Fairly quickly, as soon as the first sign of a problem appears.
Only when an error message pops up or a task fails.
I rarely notice small problems; I rely on others to point them out.

question: How do you arrange the steps of a complex project plan?

options.text: I arrange them in a highly logical order to save time and work efficiently.
I follow a standard, proven order, making small changes if needed.
I list steps as I think of them and organize them later.
I struggle with step-by-step planning; I prefer to work on things as they come up.

question: How easily can you reorganize your files or data into completely different categories?

options.text: Very easily—I can instantly see many different ways to group the same files.
With some thought, I can reorganize them to fit a new system.
I find it boring and prefer to stick to my original system.
Very confusing—changing categories ruins my entire setup.

question: How often do your ideas surprise your classmates or coworkers?

options.text: Often—I think creatively and suggest highly unusual ideas.
Sometimes, though my ideas are usually based on existing plans.
Rarely—I prefer standard, dependable ways that are known to work.
Never—I stick strictly to standard rules and traditional methods.

question: How easily can you mentally picture and rotate a 3D object or layout?

options.text: Very easily—I can mentally turn and build complex 3D layouts in detail.
Fairly well; sketching it on paper helps me visualize it.
Only basic shapes; complex structures quickly get confusing.
I struggle; I cannot easily visualize 3D objects or structures.

question: How easily can you remember a long list of terms, shortcuts, or codes?

options.text: Very easily—I have a strong memory for names, codes, and facts.
Well enough, though I prefer to look them up to be safe.
Slowly—I need to see them many times over days to remember them.
Poorly—I rely completely on notes, bookmarks, and search bars.

question: How easily can you understand a difficult academic paper or legal contract?

options.text: Easily—I quickly understand hard words and the main idea of difficult texts.
With focus, though I sometimes have to reread parts to understand them.
Slowly—I need a simple summary or guide to help me read it.
I struggle—I find highly technical or dense writing very hard to read.

question: How quickly can your eyes spot a tiny mistake on a busy screen or data list?

options.text: Almost instantly—I can scan a pattern and spot a mistake immediately.
Fairly fast, as long as I am checking systematically row-by-row.
At a moderate pace; I do not like to rush because I might miss details.
Slowly—I take a lot of time to look at and compare things.

question: How easily can you block out background noise when working in a busy place?

options.text: Completely—I can focus on my screen and tune out conversations entirely.
Fairly well, especially if I wear headphones with music.
Only for a short time; sudden noises or voices quickly break my focus.
Not at all—I need absolute silence to focus on my work.

question: What happens when you are asked to quickly list ideas or slogans for a new app?

options.text: I can come up with 20 or more creative ideas in just a few minutes.
I can make a list of 5 to 10 good ideas after brainstorming.
I can think of 2 or 3 basic ideas, but then I get stuck.
I struggle—I prefer to work on one idea rather than think of many.

question: How easily can you explain a technical concept to people who are not technical?

options.text: Very easily—I speak clearly, use simple examples, and keep people interested.
Fairly well, though I sometimes use technical words that confuse people.
With some difficulty—I know the idea, but finding the right words is hard.
I struggle—I prefer explaining things in writing rather than speaking.

question: How easily can you spot patterns in a messy set of data or feedback?

options.text: Very easily—I naturally see trends and connect scattered details into a clear pattern.
With some thought, I can connect the dots and find the common thread.
Slowly—I prefer when someone else tells me what the pattern might be.
I struggle—disorganized data just looks like random noise to me.

question: How do you calculate math like percentages or budgets during a discussion?

options.text: I can calculate percentages or estimates in my head instantly.
I write the math on paper quickly to check my estimate.
I use a calculator or spreadsheet to avoid making mistakes.
I ask someone else to check the math; I am not good at mental math.

question: How easily can you follow a complex spoken explanation of a problem?

options.text: Very easily—I understand spoken info quickly, follow the steps, and remember key points.
Fairly well, though I take notes to make sure I do not miss anything.
Slowly—I easily lose track of spoken details and need people to repeat things.
I struggle—I need to see things written down or drawn to understand them.

question: How easily can you write a report, summary, or blog post?

options.text: Very easily—I write clearly, organize my thoughts logically, and write quickly.
Fairly well, though I spend a lot of time editing my writing.
With some difficulty—putting my thoughts into clear writing takes a lot of effort.
I struggle—I strongly prefer speaking or using bullet points over formal writing.
